interactions.reason: Both increase bleeding risk. Concurrent use significantly raises risk of major hemorrhage including GI and intracranial bleeding.
Minor — metformin may slightly enhance warfarin effect. Monitor INR if starting/stopping.
ACE inhibitors may enhance hypoglycemic effect of metformin. Monitor blood glucose.
Aspirin may reduce antihypertensive effect of lisinopril and increase risk of renal impairment.
Beta-blockers can mask hypoglycemia symptoms in diabetic patients. Educate patient on alternative warning signs.
Atorvastatin may increase warfarin effect. Monitor INR closely when initiating or adjusting statin dose.
Additive hypotensive effect. Monitor blood pressure, especially with sublingual nitro use.
Additive hypotensive effect. Risk of symptomatic hypotension.
NSAIDs reduce ACE inhibitor efficacy. May worsen renal function in elderly or volume-depleted patients.
Both anticoagulants. Bridging therapy intentional in ACS but requires careful INR + aPTT monitoring.
Significantly increased bleeding risk. Common in ACS protocols but requires close monitoring of platelets and Hgb.
Risk of hypotension and acute kidney injury, especially on initiation. Monitor BP, BUN, creatinine, electrolytes.
Diuretic may worsen renal function; reduced metformin clearance increases lactic acidosis risk in renal impairment.
Generally safe. Mild CYP interaction, clinically rarely significant.
